services.name: Presto
services.description: Meta's primary interactive SQL query engine for ad-hoc analytics on the data warehouse. Presto runs distributed queries across Hive-partitioned tables stored in HDFS or S3-compatible object storage at petabyte scale.
keyFeatures: Massively parallel query execution across hundreds of nodes
Supports complex window functions, CTEs, QUALIFY clause
Federated queries across Hive, MySQL, Kafka, and other connectors
Cost-based optimizer for join ordering and predicate pushdown
No data movement — queries run in-place on storage
commonUseCases: Ad-hoc analytics and exploration on petabyte-scale tables
Dashboard queries served from pre-aggregated Hive tables
Cross-database joins for data reconciliation
Data quality checks and profiling on large partitions
services.interviewTips: Know how to write QUALIFY to filter window function results without a subquery. Understand partition pruning — always filter on the partition key (usually event_date) first. Know the difference between Presto and Hive: Presto is interactive (seconds to minutes), Hive is batch (minutes to hours). Mention predicate pushdown and why column pruning matters for ORC/Parquet files.

services.name: Hive
services.description: Meta's batch SQL execution engine for large-scale ETL and data warehouse pipelines. Hive translates SQL queries into MapReduce or Tez jobs that process data in HDFS-backed partitioned tables.
keyFeatures: Partitioned table support with partition pruning
ORC and Parquet columnar storage formats
ACID transactions for INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE on managed tables
Static and dynamic partition overwrite for idempotent writes
HiveQL supports most standard SQL including window functions
commonUseCases: Nightly ETL batch jobs writing to warehouse tables
Historical data backfills and partition-level rewrites
Large aggregations that exceed Presto's memory limits
Data compaction and table maintenance jobs
services.interviewTips: Know INSERT OVERWRITE for idempotent partition writes — this is how you make pipelines safe to re-run. Understand the difference between external and managed tables (dropping a managed table deletes data). Know how ORC file statistics enable partition and file-level pruning. Mention that Hive on Tez is significantly faster than Hive on MapReduce.

services.name: Apache Spark
services.description: Meta's distributed data processing framework for large-scale ETL, machine learning feature pipelines, and streaming data processing. Spark Structured Streaming handles real-time event processing with exactly-once semantics.
keyFeatures: In-memory distributed computation — significantly faster than Hive for iterative algorithms
Structured Streaming for real-time event processing with watermarking
DataFrames API in Python, Scala, and SQL
MLlib for distributed machine learning
Catalyst query optimizer and Tungsten execution engine
commonUseCases: Real-time event deduplication and sessionization
Graph algorithm processing (connected components, PageRank)
ML feature engineering pipelines at scale
Data lake compaction and small file consolidation
services.interviewTips: Know the difference between transformations (lazy, build the DAG) and actions (trigger execution). Understand why shuffles are expensive and how to minimize them with proper partitioning. For streaming, know what watermarking does — it bounds late-data handling and controls state store size. Know when to use foreachBatch to write to multiple sinks. Explain RDD vs DataFrame vs Dataset.

services.name: Scribe
services.description: Meta's internal high-throughput log aggregation and event streaming system. Scribe collects billions of structured events per day from client apps and server-side services, routes them to appropriate consumers, and persists them to HDFS for batch processing.
keyFeatures: At-least-once delivery semantics (consumers must handle deduplication)
Structured event schemas enforced at production time
Multiple consumer groups can read the same stream independently
Automatic routing based on stream category and retention policies
Real-time consumer latency of seconds, batch consumer latency of minutes
commonUseCases: Client-side event logging (clicks, impressions, page views)
Server-side metrics and audit logs
Real-time pipeline triggers for stream processing jobs
Feed into analytics warehouse via Hive batch jobs
services.interviewTips: Scribe is analogous to Kafka in the open-source world — mention this equivalence in interviews. Key concept: at-least-once delivery means your consumer must implement idempotent deduplication using event_id. Know the difference between real-time consumers (Spark Streaming, Flink) and batch consumers (Hive jobs reading from Scribe archives). Pipeline SLAs depend on which consumption mode you choose.

services.name: dbt (Data Build Tool)
services.description: The SQL-based transformation framework used for building and testing the analytics layer of the data warehouse. dbt models define transformations as SELECT statements, and dbt handles materialization, dependency resolution, and testing.
keyFeatures: SQL-native transformations — no Python or JVM required for most use cases
Incremental materialization strategies: append, merge (UPSERT), delete+insert
Built-in testing: not_null, unique, accepted_values, referential integrity
Jinja templating for dynamic SQL generation and macros
Auto-generated documentation and lineage DAG
commonUseCases: Staging models that clean and normalize raw source data
Intermediate models for complex business logic transformations
Mart models that serve BI tools and dashboards
Incremental pipelines for near-real-time data freshness
services.interviewTips: Know the three materialization types: table (full refresh each run), view (query runs at read time), incremental (only process new/changed rows). For incremental, understand the unique_key config — it tells dbt to MERGE rather than APPEND. Know how SCD Type 2 is handled using dbt snapshots (strategy: timestamp or check). Mention the ref() function for dependency management — dbt builds the DAG automatically from refs.

services.name: Airflow
services.description: The workflow orchestration platform used to schedule, monitor, and manage data pipeline DAGs. Airflow defines pipeline dependencies as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) of tasks, with built-in support for retries, SLA monitoring, and backfilling.
keyFeatures: DAG-based pipeline definition in Python
Task-level retries with configurable backoff
Backfill support for re-running historical date ranges
Sensor operators for waiting on external conditions (file arrival, API response)
Extensive operator library: SQL, Spark, dbt, S3, HTTP, and custom operators
commonUseCases: Scheduling nightly ETL pipeline runs with dependency management
Triggering dbt runs after source data lands
Coordinating multi-step data processing workflows
Monitoring SLA adherence and alerting on pipeline delays
services.interviewTips: Understand the difference between schedule_interval and execution_date — execution_date is the START of the interval, not when the task runs. Know how to implement idempotent tasks using OVERWRITE semantics so backfills are safe. Explain the XCom mechanism for passing data between tasks (and why it should only be used for small values, not entire DataFrames). Know when to use a Sensor vs. a dependency trigger.

services.name: Delta Lake / Apache Hudi
services.description: ACID-compliant storage layers built on top of Parquet that enable record-level updates, deletes, and time travel on data lakes. Delta Lake (Databricks) and Apache Hudi (Uber/Meta) solve the mutability problem in traditional data lakes.
keyFeatures: ACID transactions: concurrent reads and writes are safe
Record-level UPSERT and DELETE — eliminates the need for full partition rewrites
Time travel: query the table as it was at any point in history
Schema enforcement and evolution with safe alter operations
Automatic small file compaction
commonUseCases: CDC (Change Data Capture) pipelines that need to apply row-level updates
Late-arriving data that updates previously written records
GDPR right-to-be-forgotten (delete specific user records without full rewrite)
Building mutable fact tables for the identity graph or session data
services.interviewTips: Understand why traditional Parquet/ORC on Hive does not support row-level updates — you must rewrite entire partitions. Delta Lake and Hudi solve this with a transaction log (Delta) or copy-on-write/merge-on-read modes (Hudi). Know the trade-off: write amplification (copy-on-write is slower to write, faster to read) vs. read amplification (merge-on-read is faster to write, slower to read). Mention when you would choose each mode.

services.name: Snowflake
services.description: A cloud-native data warehouse with separate compute and storage scaling. Snowflake supports multi-cluster virtual warehouses, zero-copy cloning, and a broad SQL dialect. Commonly used at companies before migrating to internal big data infrastructure.
keyFeatures: Separate compute and storage: scale each independently
Multi-cluster warehouses for concurrent workload isolation
Zero-copy cloning for instant table snapshots without data duplication
Time travel (up to 90 days) for historical queries and undoing mistakes
Supports semi-structured data (JSON, Avro, Parquet) via VARIANT type
commonUseCases: Analytics data warehouse for BI tools and dashboards
Staging area for data before transformation (ELT pattern)
Cross-cloud data sharing between organizations
Development and test environments using zero-copy clones
services.interviewTips: Know the difference between virtual warehouse sizes (XS to 6XL) and when to scale up vs. scale out. Understand the SnowflakeManager MERGE pattern for idempotent UPSERTs. Know how clustering keys work — analogous to Hive partitioning but uses micro-partition metadata rather than physical directory structure. Explain why Snowflake's separation of compute and storage eliminates the need to pre-allocate cluster resources.

services.name: Apache Kafka
services.description: The open-source distributed event streaming platform equivalent to Meta's Scribe. Kafka provides high-throughput, low-latency, fault-tolerant publish-subscribe messaging for real-time data pipelines.
keyFeatures: Partitioned topics for horizontal scalability
Consumer groups for parallel processing with offset tracking
Configurable retention (time-based or size-based)
Exactly-once semantics via idempotent producers and transactional consumers
Kafka Streams for lightweight stream processing co-located with brokers
commonUseCases: Real-time event ingestion from web and mobile clients
Decoupling microservices with async messaging
Change Data Capture stream from databases (Debezium connector)
Log aggregation and metrics collection
services.interviewTips: Know the difference between at-least-once and exactly-once delivery — exactly-once requires both idempotent producer AND transactional consumer. Understand how partition count affects parallelism (one consumer per partition maximum). Explain the consumer group offset commit strategy: auto-commit vs. manual commit. Know why Kafka retention is not the same as a database — Kafka is for streaming, not querying. Mention the role of the schema registry for enforcing Avro/Protobuf schemas.

services.name: Apache Flink
services.description: A stateful distributed stream processing framework for low-latency event processing. Flink provides true streaming semantics (vs. Spark's micro-batch approach) and is well-suited for complex event processing, sessionization, and exactly-once guarantees.
keyFeatures: True streaming: processes events one at a time, not in micro-batches
Stateful operators with managed state backends (RocksDB, memory)
Event time processing with watermarks for handling out-of-order events
Exactly-once semantics via checkpointing and two-phase commit
Table API and SQL support for familiar query patterns
commonUseCases: Real-time fraud detection with complex pattern matching
Sessionization and user behavior analysis on streaming events
Real-time feature computation for ML model serving
CDC stream processing with record-level transformations
services.interviewTips: Explain the difference between Flink's true streaming and Spark's micro-batch approach — Flink has lower latency but higher operational complexity. Know what a checkpoint is in Flink — it is a consistent snapshot of all operator states that enables recovery to exactly the last checkpoint on failure. Understand watermarks: they tell Flink how far behind real time the event time data is, and when it is safe to emit window results. Know when to use processing time vs. event time.
